name: OIDC Support
description: Add an OpenID Connect layer (ID tokens, UserInfo, discovery, JWKS) on top of the existing OAuth 2.0 authorization code grant.
why: Most teams searching for a TypeScript OAuth2 server actually want OIDC — they just don't know the distinction. Today the library answers OAuth questions but not authentication questions. Without ID tokens, a UserInfo endpoint, discovery, and a JWKS endpoint, it cannot be a relying party's login provider, which is what the majority of real-world auth integrations need.
outcome: A consumer can enable OIDC by supplying an asymmetric signing key and an OIDC config block. Relying parties can run the PKCE authorization code flow with scope=openid, receive a verifiable ID token, fetch user claims from UserInfo, and auto-configure via the discovery and JWKS endpoints. For non-OIDC consumers the token wire format is byte-identical to today; the only behavior change is JwtService.verify pinning the configured algorithm — a safe-by-default hardening that rejects algorithm-confused tokens. Documented in the upgrade guide.
in_scope: ID token issuance in the authorization code flow when the openid scope is granted
UserInfo endpoint returning scope-derived claims for a presented access token, with iss validation and a recorded aud policy
JWKS endpoint publishing the public signing key
Asymmetric RS256 token signing with a consumer-supplied RSA keypair
nonce capture and propagation into the ID token, with a fail-loud guard when an opaque code loses nonce in transit
Standard OIDC scope-to-claim mapping (OIDC Core §5.4) and scope-gated filtering for openid, profile, email, address, and phone
getUserClaims and optional getIdTokenClaims consumer hooks
A named AccessTokenVerifier seam that owns iss/aud/typ/alg checks regardless of which JwtInterface the consumer supplies
out_of_scope: Implicit and hybrid flows — response_type=code only
FAPI 2.0 profiles, the claims request parameter, JAR (request/request_uri), and response_mode beyond query
Pairwise subject identifiers — public subject type only
ID token issuance on the refresh_token grant
Multi-key rotation machinery — kid headers and JWKS-as-array keep it a future additive change
Runtime key generation — keypair lifecycle is the consumer's responsibility

ES256 (and any non-RS256 signing) in v1 — OIDC Discovery §3 requires RS256 in id_token_signing_alg_values_supported; a single ES256 key cannot satisfy this. ES256 is a future additive change (a one-line capability addition once a multi-key story exists).
Formal OpenID conformance suite in CI
Dynamic client registration and RP-initiated / front-channel / back-channel logout

use_cases.description: A relying party runs the PKCE authorization code flow with scope=openid and a nonce, exchanges the code at the token endpoint, and receives an ID token alongside the access token.
A relying party verifies an issued ID token's signature against the keys published at the JWKS endpoint.
A relying party presents an access token to the UserInfo endpoint and receives the end-user's scope-derived claims.
A relying party fetches the discovery document to learn the server's OIDC capabilities and endpoint URLs.

A consumer enables OIDC by supplying an asymmetric signing key and OIDC config; a consumer who does not enable OIDC sees a byte-identical token wire format to today, with the single behavior change that verify() now pins the configured algorithm — documented in the upgrade guide.
A consumer adds custom claims such as roles, tenant, or acr to issued ID tokens via the getIdTokenClaims hook.
A consumer using opaque authorization codes runs the OIDC flow with nonce binding preserved across the authorize-to-token round trip.

decisions: v1 ships RS256 only; ES256 is deferred (a single ES256 key cannot satisfy the OIDC Discovery §3 RS256-mandatory requirement). One consumer-supplied RSA keypair signs both ID and access tokens when OIDC is enabled; the symmetric HS256 path is unchanged for non-OIDC users (ADR 0001).
No new runtime dependency: jsonwebtoken plus Node's built-in crypto cover signing, JWK export, and thumbprint computation.
The consumer supplies the private key only (PEM string or KeyObject); the library derives the public key and never generates keys at runtime.
v1 ships a single signing key; every token header carries a kid; the JWKS endpoint always publishes a keys array; kid defaults to the RFC 7638 thumbprint and is overridable.

The three OIDC endpoints (UserInfo, discovery, JWKS) are added as methods on AuthorizationServer; ID token issuance lives inside the authorization code grant; there is no separate provider class (ADR 0002).
OIDC is enabled by an explicit oidc config block on AuthorizationServerOptions; an ID token is issued per-request only when the granted scopes include openid.

When OIDC is enabled the library auto-recognizes openid, profile, email, address, and phone as valid. offline_access is not auto-recognized in v1 because refresh-token issuance is consumer-owned and no ID token is issued on refresh.

A getUserClaims callback supplies user attributes; the default ID token stays lean (protocol claims only); scope-derived claims are served only from UserInfo; the library owns the standard scope-to-claim mapping and filtering.
getUserClaims is keyed by the user identifier, not a loaded user object, so the OIDC config needs no user repository.
The library owns nonce end-to-end; all other OIDC authorization parameters are parsed and exposed to the consumer but not acted on; prompt=none is pass-through.
auth_time is consumer-supplied on the authorization request; the library throws when max_age was requested but auth_time is absent.
Only response_type=code is supported; no implicit or hybrid flows.
Refresh-token issuance is not gated by the library; offline_access handling is left to the consumer's token repository.
The refresh flow stays OIDC-unaware; no ID token is issued on refresh.

Discovery requires explicit endpoint URLs from the consumer; issuer is reused from options and mandatory under OIDC; remaining fields are auto-derived; an optional metadata override is shallow-merged in.
Both opaque and JWT authorization codes are supported under OIDC; nonce and auth_time are added as optional fields on the auth code entity and payload, propagated by both encoders.
JwtInterface gains an optional JWKS-exposing method; OIDC construction throws if the supplied JWT service lacks it.
ID token claims: iss, stringified sub, aud set to the client, exp reusing the access-token TTL, iat, conditional nonce and auth_time, and at_hash; azp is omitted in v1.
An optional getIdTokenClaims hook merges custom claims into the ID token; protocol claims always win and cannot be overridden.

UserInfo requires the openid scope, returns plain JSON, and routes verification through a named AccessTokenVerifier seam in src/oidc/ that owns the algorithm pin, typ:at+jwt check, iss equality check, and the recorded aud policy — regardless of which JwtInterface the consumer supplies. Verification also checks signature, expiry, and revocation when getByAccessToken is implemented.

UserInfo aud policy for v1: accept tokens issued by this server (iss match) without further aud validation, and record this as the documented limitation; a future resource-server-scoped audience check is a forward-compatible addition once the audience parameter has a discovery story.

OIDC access tokens carry the JOSE header typ:at+jwt (RFC 9068) while ID tokens keep typ:JWT; UserInfo rejects any non-at+jwt token, preventing an ID token from being accepted as a bearer access token (ADR 0003).
JwtService.verify pins the verification algorithm to the single configured algorithm, so a token's own alg header can never drive verification (algorithm-confusion defense, ADR 0003).
The OIDC construction guard validates issuer is a well-formed absolute https URL with no query or fragment; http is permitted only for loopback hosts.
A single canonical stringify (String(user.id)) produces sub for the ID token, the UserInfo response, and the getUserClaims key, keeping sub byte-identical across all three.
The discovery document advertises code_challenge_methods_supported as ["S256"] only; the grant still accepts a plain code_challenge_method but does not advertise it.
The getIdTokenClaims protocol-claims-win merge protects the full Protocol Claims set including the conditional nonce and auth_time.

JWT auth codes are the recommended encoder for OIDC consumers; opaque-code consumers must persist the new nonce/auth_time fields. If an opaque-code payload is resolved at token time and nonce is missing while the authorization request carried one, the library fails loud (throws invalid_grant) rather than minting a nonce-less ID token — silent loss is treated as a security failure, not a doc note.
max_age freshness is enforced: when max_age was requested and auth_time + max_age < now, the library throws an OAuthException; consumers are not trusted to enforce freshness themselves.
risks: JwtService.sign currently drops its options argument; it must be fixed to forward algorithm and keyid — a latent bug touched by this work.
Asymmetric signing changes the token format for OIDC consumers (kid header, RS256/ES256); the existing symmetric HS256 path must remain byte-identical so non-OIDC users see no breaking change.
AuthorizationServerOptions is currently flat scalars; nesting an oidc object containing callbacks is a shape change to a public interface — additive but notable.
Consumers using opaque authorization codes must persist two new entity fields (nonce, auth_time) or OIDC nonce binding silently breaks; this requires a clear doc note.
This is a published library — every change must be additive and no public signature may change.

JwtService.verify will pin algorithms for existing symmetric consumers too (["HS256"]); a safe-by-default tightening of verification behavior. A caller-supplied algorithms VerifyOptions is now silently ignored — this is a behavior change requiring a CHANGELOG entry and an upgrade-guide note, not just inline docs.

Asymmetric signing is ~100× slower than HS256 per operation. For high-throughput deployments this is a real operational consideration; accepted-and-documented in v1 since the keypair is fixed and signing is amortized over short access-token lifetimes.

The JwtInterface contract cannot enforce algorithm pinning, typ:at+jwt, or JWK export — a consumer who supplies a custom JwtInterface would otherwise bypass these security properties. The AccessTokenVerifier seam in src/oidc/ owns the security checks regardless of the injected JWT service, closing this gap.
validation: Unit tests: JWK export, RFC 7638 thumbprint kid against a known-answer vector, RS256 sign-and-verify round trip, scope-to-claim filtering (OIDC Core §5.4 exact), discovery document generation, ID token claim assembly, and at_hash against the OIDC Core §A.3 known-answer test vector.

Unit tests: algorithm-confusion attack rejection — HS256 token forged using the RSA public key as the HMAC secret is rejected; alg:none is rejected; malformed PEM and public-only keys fail at construction.

Keystone e2e (vanilla adapter): authorize → token → independently verify the issued ID token using the `jose` library against the live /jwks endpoint (not the library's own JwtService — a self-referential verify cannot catch a JWK-export or thumbprint bug) → call /userinfo → assert decoded id_token.sub equals userinfo sub.

e2e: opaque-authorization-code OIDC variant; 403 insufficient_scope when openid scope is absent at UserInfo; 401 invalid_token when an ID token is presented as a bearer access token; iss mismatch rejection.
Regression: byte-identical HS256 JOSE-header snapshot for non-OIDC consumers — assert `{alg:HS256,typ:JWT}` exactly, with no kid and no at+jwt. Snapshot, not round-trip.

Timer strategy for the keystone e2e is specified upfront: use real timers via vi.useRealTimers() inside the describe block, since test/setup.ts installs fake timers globally and a JWKS verify against `iat`/`exp` cannot work under frozen time.
Manual smoke test against openid-client; gate release on the automated jose-library verification e2e, not the manual smoke.

Dedicated resilience pass: cross-cutting negative e2e coverage for algorithm confusion, alg:none, wrong typ, wrong issuer, expired/revoked tokens, missing openid scope, JWKS/discovery cache headers, and opaque-code nonce loss.
notes: Implementation phases in dependency order. Phase 1: key service and JWKS — extend JwtService for asymmetric keys, fix sign() to forward options, add a JWKS-exposing method to JwtInterface. Phase 2: the oidc config block on AuthorizationServerOptions plus fail-fast construction guards (issuer set, JWT service exposes JWKS). Phase 3: ID token issuance inside the authorization code grant — nonce and auth_time threaded through the authorization request, auth code entity, payload, and both auth code encoders. Phase 4: the UserInfo endpoint, the claims resolver and standard scope-to-claim mapping, and new invalid_token / insufficient_scope error types. Phase 5: discovery, example-app persistence, and cross-cutting resilience/conformance validation. Phase 6: public API audit and docs integration. New code is concentrated under src/oidc/. The framework adapters need no changes — the three new endpoints return a plain ResponseInterface that every adapter already handles. Rough size: 1,300-1,600 lines of library code and 900-1,200 lines of tests. Three ADRs are already recorded: docs/adr/0001 (asymmetric signing), docs/adr/0002 (OIDC layered on the auth code grant), and docs/adr/0003 (token verification hardening). Domain glossary captured in CONTEXT.md.
